fn1: Ceremony where the bride chooses her own groom. He told them that the svayamvara was being planned.
fn2: Yajnasena is one of Drupada’s names.
fn8: A brahmachari. Because he had been a brahmachari, he had immense powers.
fn9: Drona. A drona was used to carry water and can also be translated as a vessel, rather than cup.
fn10: The son of Prishata, hence Drupada.
fn12: That is, going away to the forest after giving away all his wealth.
fn16: Chhatravati is another name for Ahichhatra, which was the capital of the kingdom of Panchala.
fn18: As will become evident later, Drupada’s wife accompanied him on his travels.
fn20: The word snataka has many nuances. Here it means a Brahmana who has completed his ﬁrst course of study.
fn24: This inconsistency in the number of cows offered to Yaja exists in the text.
fn26: A qualiﬁcation is needed for this translation. The word used is mithuna, and what seems to be suggested is an act of intercourse between the queen and Yaja. One can also translate this sentence as, ‘The time for the twins has arrived’, meaning the births of Droupadi and Dhrishtadyumna, but this doesn’t seem to be very natural as a translation.
fn28: Dhrishtata means courage and conﬁdence, and dyumna means splendour, glory or lustre.
fn37: Kunapa. This can also mean those who smell like corpses and is therefore also used as a contemptuous term for those who are alive.
fn38: Shatakratu is a performer of 100 sacriﬁces and is one of Indra’s names.
fn39: There was no danger from this revelation of identity, since it wouldn’t have reached the Kouravas.
fn40: One with an adorned chariot; ratha is chariot.
fn41: One whose chariot has been burnt.
fn42: Chakshu means eye. That is the reason the knowledge is called chakshushi.
fn43: The word used in the text is yaviyasa, which means Shudra.
fn44: The word vadava means a mare.
fn45: This is now a reference to the horses the gandharva is giving.
fn46: Bibhatsu is one of Arjuna’s names.
fn47: An explanation is needed about the giving of a weapon. This act of giving doesn’t involve the physical act of giving something and has more to do with teaching the mantras required to release and withdraw a weapon. Therefore, giving a weapon away did not mean that the giver ceased to possess it. What was being given was the knowledge.
fn48: Keep ﬁre burning in the household.
fn51: The next sentence makes it clear that this is the sun-god.
fn53: The one with many rays; a name for the sun.
fn55: Another name for Lakshmi, the goddess of wealth.
fn56: The word used is rambha, which means a plantain tree. Thus, one with thighs like a plantain tree. However, Rambha is also the name of a beautiful apsara and one can also translate this as one with thighs like those of Rambha.
fn58: This can mean different things. It can mean a rainbow, described as Indra’s bow (Shakra is another name for Indra), when the time is right for a rainbow. Alternatively, it can mean a ﬂag erected when the time is right for Indra’s festival.
fn65: Gopati means lord of cows and is another name for the sun.
fn72: Vishvamitra and his sons are known as the Kushikas. Kushika was Vishvamitra’s grandfather. According to some accounts, Kushika was Vishvamitra’s father.
fn73: The six different tastes are katu (pungent), amla (sour), madhur (sweet), lavana (salty), tikta (bitter) and kashaya (astringent).
fn75: In this shloka, the cow’s name is given as Nandi. However, Nandini is more common and subsequent shlokas say Nandini.
fn79: Often used as a term for the Persians.
fn83: Mountainous tribes who lived through hunting.
fn85: Identiﬁed with the inhabitants of Sri Lanka.
fn87: Inhabitants of a country to the north.
fn88: Barbarians, non-Aryans, those who did not speak Sanskrit.
fn90: Nectar, ambrosia, the beverage of the gods.
fn91: In the sense that the king should have yielded the way to a Brahmana.
fn96: Mitrasaha can also be translated as ‘with his friends’. However, no friends have been mentioned. So Mitrasaha is more likely to be one of Kalmashapada’s names.
fn97: Pasha means noose or bond and Vipasha is something that frees one from a bond. Vipasha is identiﬁed with the river Beas.
fn98: Himavati can be the speciﬁc name for a river. Alternatively, it can also be a general term for any river descending from the Himalayas.
fn99: Shatadru is a river with a hundred streams and is identiﬁed as the river Sutlej.
fn100: This is a reference to the six Vedangas.
fn101: To his hermitage. The unblemished one is Vashishtha.
fn105: The word used is sandhya, which can mean dawn, as well as dusk.
fn106: This story is taken up later, and we learn why Kalmashapada requested Vashishtha to impregnate the queen.
fn108: Meaning the king, who had now been freed from the curse of the rakshasa.
fn114: There are fourteen lokas (worlds). Seven are above the ground and seven are below. This is a reference to the seven that are above the ground—bhurloka, bhuvarloka, svarloka, maharloka, janarloka, taparloka and brahmaloka (satyaloka).
fn115: That is, unless something is done about the anger.
fn117: A horse is vadava and a horse’s head is called vadavamukha, mukha meaning face. This submarine ﬁre is thus called vadavamukha. Since anala means ﬁre, it is also called vadavanala.
fn119: Because they had nothing to do with Shakti’s death.
